title: Identification
controlText: Identify information system users, processes acting on behalf of users, or devices.
plainEnglish: Every user, automated process, and device on your systems must have a unique identifier — no shared logins, no anonymous accounts, no unidentified devices on the network. Identification is the prerequisite that makes accountability possible. You cannot enforce access control or produce useful audit logs without it.

objectives.text: system users are identified;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Each human user has a unique account. No shared logins for active work. A documented naming convention is applied consistently. Generic accounts (admin, guest, test) are disabled or have a documented exception with compensating controls.
commonEvidence: Evidence that shared and default accounts are disabled or accounted for

objectives.text: processes acting on behalf of users are identified; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Service accounts have unique names, a documented owner, and a documented purpose. Scripts and automation run under named service identities, not under a real human user's account.
commonEvidence: Service account inventory with owner and purpose
Service principal documentation for cloud workloads

objectives.text: devices accessing the system are identified.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Devices have unique identifiers (hostname, MAC address, certificate, asset tag) recorded in an inventory. Unknown devices on the network are detected and investigated.

commonGaps: Shared admin or service accounts with no individual ownership
Default accounts (admin, root, guest) still enabled
Service accounts with no documented owner — nobody knows what would break if disabled
Personal/BYOD devices on the corporate network without identification
No naming convention — accounts created ad hoc
Multiple users sharing one mailbox or license to save money
Devices identified by IP only, with no inventory mapping IP to owner
Inventory exists in a spreadsheet that is updated manually and is months out of date

title: Authentication
controlText: Authenticate (or verify) the identities of those users, processes, or devices, as a prerequisite to allowing access to organizational information systems.
plainEnglish: Identifying who someone (or something) claims to be is only step one. Authentication is proving it — usually via password, certificate, key, or token. Default credentials must be changed before a system goes into use, and the strength of authentication should match the sensitivity of what is being protected. Note: this control covers basic authentication; MFA is addressed separately under IA.L2-3.5.3.

objectives.text: the identity of each user is authenticated or verified as a prerequisite to system access;
objectives.whatToLookFor: All in-scope systems require login before access. A password policy meeting NIST 800-63B guidance is enforced. Default passwords have been changed on every device, appliance, and application. No anonymous access to in-scope systems.
commonEvidence: Records of default credential changes (change tickets, configuration screenshots)

objectives.text: the identity of each process acting on behalf of a user is authenticated or verified as a prerequisite to system access; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Service accounts use strong, rotated credentials or certificate/key-based authentication. API calls require keys or tokens. No hardcoded plaintext credentials in scripts, config files, or repositories.
commonEvidence: Secrets management tool inventory (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault)
Code review evidence or secrets-scanning results showing no hardcoded credentials

objectives.text: the identity of each device accessing or connecting to the system is authenticated or verified as a prerequisite to system access.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Device authentication via certificates, 802.1X, or compliant-device conditional access. Wireless requires WPA2/WPA3 Enterprise or equivalent — no open SSIDs and no PSK-only networks for corporate access to in-scope systems.
commonEvidence: Conditional access policies requiring compliant or hybrid-joined device

commonArtifacts: Default credential change records or change tickets
commonGaps: Default passwords still in place on switches, printers, IoT devices, or appliances
Weak password policy not meeting current NIST 800-63B guidance
Hardcoded credentials in scripts, config files, or source repositories
Service account passwords never rotated, even after admins leave
No device authentication on internal networks — anything plugged in works
Open or PSK-only Wi-Fi used for corporate access to in-scope systems
Shared service account credentials known to multiple admins with no rotation when staff change
Authentication enforced at the front door but bypassable through internal east-west traffic

title: Multifactor Authentication
controlText: Use multifactor authentication for local and network access to privileged accounts and for network access to non-privileged accounts.
plainEnglish: MFA is required in three places: (1) local access to privileged accounts (admin signing in at the console), (2) network access to privileged accounts (admin signing in remotely), and (3) network access to non-privileged accounts (any user signing in over the network). The combination of factors must be drawn from: something you know, something you have, something you are. SMS-based MFA is increasingly discouraged in favor of authenticator apps, hardware tokens, or FIDO2/WebAuthn.

objectives.text: privileged accounts are identified;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Same inventory used for AC.L2-3.1.5 — every privileged account documented and known.

objectives.text: multifactor authentication is implemented for local access to privileged accounts;
objectives.whatToLookFor: MFA enforced even when an admin is at the console — typically smart card, hardware token, or biometric. This is the often-missed objective; many organizations enforce MFA for remote access only.
commonEvidence: Smart card or token configuration for local privileged login
Audit logs showing local privileged logins required MFA

objectives.text: multifactor authentication is implemented for network access to privileged accounts; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: MFA enforced for every network-based privileged login — VPN, RDP, SSH bastion, cloud admin console. No exceptions for 'internal' access to admin tools.
commonEvidence: Conditional access policies requiring MFA for admin roles

objectives.text: multifactor authentication is implemented for network access to non-privileged accounts.
objectives.whatToLookFor: MFA enforced for all users accessing systems over the network — VPN, web applications, cloud SaaS containing CUI. Conditional access policies make this enforceable across cloud services.
commonEvidence: Conditional access policies for all users

commonArtifacts: Smart card or token deployment records for local privileged access
commonGaps: MFA enforced for remote access but not for local privileged login at the console
SMS-based MFA used despite NIST 800-63B guidance against it
MFA exceptions granted for 'service accounts' or 'legacy systems' that handle CUI
Conditional access policies have unintended exclusions
Cloud-based mailbox accessed via legacy authentication protocols that bypass MFA

title: Replay-Resistant Authentication
controlText: Employ replay-resistant authentication mechanisms for network access to privileged and non-privileged accounts.
plainEnglish: Authentication mechanisms must resist replay attacks — where an attacker captures network traffic containing authentication data and replays it later to gain access. Modern protocols (Kerberos, TLS, SAML with timestamped assertions, OIDC, FIDO2) build in replay resistance. Older or insecure protocols (basic HTTP auth without TLS, plain LDAP, NTLM in some configurations) do not.

objectives.text: replay-resistant authentication mechanisms are implemented for network account access to privileged and non-privileged accounts.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Authentication uses protocols with built-in replay resistance — Kerberos, TLS-protected authentication, SAML/OIDC with nonces and short-lived tokens, FIDO2/WebAuthn. Legacy protocols disabled (LM/NTLMv1, basic auth without TLS).
commonEvidence: Modern authentication enforcement in cloud services

commonArtifacts: Group Policy or registry settings disabling weak authentication
Modern authentication policy in cloud services (e.g., disabling Basic Auth in M365)
commonGaps: NTLMv1 or LM hashes still allowed in the environment
Legacy authentication protocols (POP3, IMAP, SMTP basic auth) enabled in cloud email
Custom applications use HTTP basic auth without enforcing TLS
Old SAML configurations without timestamp validation

title: Identifier Reuse
controlText: Prevent reuse of identifiers for a defined period.
plainEnglish: When a user leaves the organization or an account is deleted, that identifier (username, account ID) cannot be reassigned to a new person for a defined period — typically a year or longer. The risk being addressed: a new hire inheriting access through residual permissions tied to the old identifier, or audit logs becoming ambiguous about which person performed an action.

objectives.text: a period within which identifiers cannot be reused is defined; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented identifier reuse period — commonly 12-24 months. Defined in policy.
commonEvidence: Identifier management policy with defined reuse period

objectives.text: reuse of identifiers is prevented within the defined period.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Procedural and/or technical enforcement. New accounts for new hires use distinct identifiers (e.g., riley vs riley02) when names overlap. Disabled accounts retained, not deleted, during the reuse period to prevent reuse.
commonEvidence: Account creation procedure addressing name collisions
Identity provider configuration retaining disabled accounts

commonGaps: Disabled accounts deleted entirely after a short period — no protection against identifier reuse
No collision handling procedure — first-come-first-served on common names
Reuse period defined but not actually enforced when collisions arise
Cloud identity provider auto-purges deleted accounts before the defined period

title: Identifier Handling
controlText: Disable identifiers after a defined period of inactivity.
plainEnglish: Accounts that haven't been used in a defined period get automatically disabled. Inactive accounts are a frequent attack vector because nobody is watching them, and the legitimate owner may not notice if they're compromised. Standard practice is 30-90 days of inactivity. Implementation requires either an automated job or a policy enforcement feature in the IDP.

objectives.text: a period of inactivity after which an identifier is disabled is defined; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented inactivity threshold — commonly 30, 45, 60, or 90 days. Defined per account type if needed (e.g., shorter for privileged, longer for service accounts with infrequent use).
commonEvidence: Inactive account policy with defined threshold

objectives.text: identifiers are disabled after the defined period of inactivity.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Automated enforcement — IDP feature, scheduled script, or governance tool that disables accounts based on last login. Sample evidence showing accounts have actually been disabled per the threshold.
commonEvidence: Sample of recently-disabled accounts with timestamps

commonGaps: Threshold defined but no automated enforcement — relies on manual review that doesn't happen
Service accounts excluded with no compensating control
Privileged accounts have looser inactivity thresholds than non-privileged
Inactive accounts identified but not actually disabled — only reported

title: Password Complexity
controlText: Enforce a minimum password complexity and change of characters when new passwords are created.
plainEnglish: Passwords must meet a minimum complexity standard (length, character types) and a defined number of characters must change between old and new passwords. Note tension with current NIST 800-63B guidance, which deprecates traditional complexity rules in favor of length and breach-list checking — but CMMC v2 is based on NIST 800-171 R2, which still expects defined complexity. Set a defensible standard and document it.

objectives.text: password complexity requirements are defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented password complexity requirements — minimum length (commonly 12-15 characters), required character classes (upper, lower, digit, symbol), or alternative measures aligned with current NIST guidance.
commonEvidence: Password Policy with defined complexity requirements

objectives.text: password change of character requirements are defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Defined minimum number of characters that must differ between old and new passwords — prevents users from changing 'Password1' to 'Password2'.
commonEvidence: Password Policy specifying minimum changed characters
IDP configuration enforcing character change requirements

objectives.text: minimum password complexity requirements as defined are enforced when new passwords are created; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical enforcement at password creation time — passwords failing complexity are rejected. Verified through testing or IDP configuration review.

objectives.text: minimum password change of character requirements as defined are enforced when new passwords are created.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical enforcement of character-change requirement. May require fine-grained password policy or third-party tool depending on IDP.

commonArtifacts: Group Policy and IDP password configuration
commonGaps: Complexity policy defined but not technically enforced in all systems (e.g., Linux PAM not aligned with AD policy)
Cloud SaaS applications use independent password policies that don't match the documented standard
Character-change requirement not enforced — only complexity
Policy contradicts current NIST 800-63B without acknowledging the trade-off

title: Password Reuse
controlText: Prohibit password reuse for a specified number of generations.
plainEnglish: Users cannot reuse a recent password — typical implementations remember the last 5-24 passwords. This prevents users from cycling through 'Password1, Password2, Password1' to satisfy rotation requirements without actually changing anything meaningful. Note that this restriction does not apply to temporary passwords (covered by IA.L2-3.5.9).

objectives.text: the number of generations during which a password cannot be reused is specified; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Defined number of password generations — commonly 5, 12, or 24. Specified in policy.
commonEvidence: Password Policy specifying password history depth

objectives.text: reuse of passwords is prohibited during the specified number of generations.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical enforcement of password history. Verified through configuration screenshots and ideally through testing.
commonEvidence: Group Policy or IDP password history setting
Audit logs of password change events

commonGaps: Password history depth set too low to be meaningful
Cloud applications don't enforce password history
Self-service password reset workflow inadvertently bypasses history check
Application-specific passwords not subject to the policy

title: Temporary Passwords
controlText: Allow temporary password use for system logons with an immediate change to a permanent password.
plainEnglish: When a temporary password is issued (new account, password reset), the user must change it to a permanent password on first logon. Temporary passwords often follow patterns (Welcome2024!, ChangeMe!) that are easily guessed, and an attacker who captures one cannot use it for long. The 'immediate change' requirement is the key — first login must force the change.

objectives.text: an immediate change to a permanent password is required when a temporary password is used for system logon.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Account creation and password reset workflows force a password change on first login. The user cannot proceed without setting a new password. Verified by walking through a recent account creation.
commonEvidence: Account creation procedure with 'change at next logon' enforcement
Group Policy or IDP setting forcing first-login change
Sample evidence of temporary password being changed at first use

commonArtifacts: IDP configuration forcing change at first logon
Help desk procedures for password resets
commonGaps: Temporary passwords issued via insecure channels (email, chat) where they can be captured
Common temporary password used across all new accounts ('Welcome2024')
First-login change not enforced — temporary password works indefinitely
Self-service password reset bypasses the temporary password mechanism but uses similar weak patterns

title: Cryptographically-Protected Passwords
controlText: Store and transmit only cryptographically-protected passwords.
plainEnglish: Passwords must be stored as salted one-way cryptographic hashes — never in plaintext or reversible encryption — and must be transmitted only over encrypted channels. This applies everywhere passwords live: identity providers, applications, configuration files, backup data. Common gap: legacy applications that store passwords in reversible encryption or transmit them via unencrypted protocols.

objectives.text: passwords are cryptographically protected in storage; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: All password stores use salted one-way hashing (bcrypt, Argon2, PBKDF2, scrypt). No plaintext storage. No reversible encryption. Includes IDP, application databases, and backup data.
commonEvidence: Application security documentation specifying hashing algorithm
Code review or static analysis results showing no plaintext password storage

objectives.text: passwords are cryptographically protected in transit.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Authentication transactions occur over encrypted channels — TLS for HTTP, encrypted LDAP (LDAPS), Kerberos. No plain LDAP, no HTTP basic auth without TLS, no telnet.

commonGaps: Legacy applications store passwords in reversible encryption for 'password recovery' features
LDAP authentication used without TLS (LDAP rather than LDAPS)
Password sync tools transmit plaintext between systems
Custom applications use weak hashing (MD5, unsalted SHA-1) instead of modern algorithms

title: Obscure Feedback
plainEnglish: When users type passwords, the screen shows asterisks or dots — not the actual characters. This is shoulder-surfing protection. The control accommodates the reality that mobile devices may briefly display each character before obscuring it, due to small keyboards and high typo rates. Most modern systems handle this by default; the assessment objective is essentially confirming nothing has been changed to disable the obscuring.

objectives.text: authentication information is obscured during the authentication process.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Password fields display obscured characters (asterisks, dots, or filled circles). Mobile platforms may briefly show characters before obscuring. Verify by visual inspection on representative systems.
commonEvidence: Screenshots of login screens showing obscured input
Application security review confirming password masking
MDM policy configuration for mobile devices
objectives.expectedTagsNote: Intentionally unmapped. Obscured authenticator feedback is too specific for reusable tag-based recommendations and should be handled by direct artifact assignment.

commonGaps: Custom or legacy applications with unmasked password fields
Mobile device 'show password' toggle accessible without policy review
Network device console authentication with visible password entry
